# Yash Highvoltage Ltd (BSE SME 544310) — Valuation Read

**Framing:** This is a relative- and growth-adjusted valuation read on Yash Highvoltage, a ~₹2,100 Cr high-voltage transformer-bushing maker trading at ~54x P/E and ~11x P/B as of mid-June 2026. I assess where its multiple sits versus T&D-equipment peers, whether the premium is justified by growth/margins, what growth the multiple implies, the post-listing re-rating, available analyst views, and sensitivity to growth normalization. Findings only — no buy/sell.

**Important data caveats up front:**
- Yash is a **BSE SME** stock. Sell-side broker coverage is essentially absent — Trendlyne shows "No estimates available," and no mainstream brokerage price target exists. The only "targets" I found are from low-quality forecasting blogs (moneymintidea), which I do not treat as credible.
- Yash's own data has a **reported inconsistency**: Tickertape shows an FY24 EPS of ₹40.09 then FY25 EPS of ₹7.50 — that jump reflects a bonus/split or face-value change (FV is ₹5), not a real collapse. Treat per-share history pre-listing with caution; absolute revenue/profit figures are reliable.
- Figures are pulled across Screener, Tickertape, Trendlyne and Groww between ~9–13 June 2026; small differences across platforms reflect timing and TTM-vs-FY conventions. I flag these where they matter.

## Yash baseline (Screener/Tickertape, ~12 June 2026)
- Price ~₹735; market cap ~₹2,102 Cr; **P/E ~54** (Screener) to ~56 (Tickertape); **P/B ~11.4x** (Screener); EV/EBITDA ~30x (per ValueResearch/MarketsMojo data).
- FY26 sales ₹235 Cr, net profit ₹37 Cr, OPM ~24–25%, ROCE ~28.7%, ROE ~23.5%.
- Growth: FY24 ₹108 Cr → FY25 ₹150 Cr → FY26 ₹235 Cr revenue (~57% TTM sales growth, ~44% 5-yr CAGR). H1 FY26 revenue reportedly +78.6% YoY.
- Order book ~₹300–400 Cr; management guidance of ~35–40% CAGR over 5 years (and an aspirational "8–10x in 10 years"); Savli greenfield plant commercial production targeted H2 FY27.

## 2. Where Yash's ~54x sits, and PEG

On a **trailing P/E basis Yash is the most expensive name in the cohort except the three large-cap T&D giants** (Hitachi 149x, CG 117x, GVT&D 99x). Against the genuinely comparable smaller specialists it carries a **large premium**:
- vs Shilchar ~29x → **~1.8x** the multiple, despite Shilchar's much higher ROCE.
- vs TARIL/Voltamp ~32–36x → **~50–70% premium**.
- vs HBL ~26x → roughly **2x**, even though HBL grew faster on TTM and earns far higher ROCE.

**PEG (rough):** On ~54x P/E against ~50–58% trailing earnings CAGR, PEG ≈ **0.9–1.1** — not obviously stretched *if* that growth rate persists. But against management's own *forward* guidance of ~35–40% CAGR, PEG rises to **~1.4–1.5**, i.e. demanding. Shilchar at ~29x on 3-yr 33% revenue / 54% profit growth screens at a *lower* PEG with *better* returns, which is the crux of the relative-value gap.

**Is the premium justified by growth/margins?** Partly. Yash's ~24% OPM and ~29% ROCE are solid, and bushings are a higher-barrier niche than commodity transformers (a cited "structural global shortage of HV/RIP bushings" supports pricing power). But its ROCE is *below* Shilchar, GVT&D and HBL, and its margins are comparable-to-Shilchar rather than superior. So the premium rests mainly on (a) faster recent top-line growth and (b) scarcity/SME-momentum, not on best-in-class returns. On fundamentals alone, the multiple looks **richer than the return profile strictly warrants relative to Shilchar/HBL**.

## 3. Growth implied by the multiple

At ~54x trailing on a ~24% margin, single-digit-billion-rupee base, the market is **extrapolating several more years of 40%+ growth**, broadly in line with the bull case of ₹600–700 Cr revenue within 2–3 years (from ₹235 Cr FY26) plus margin hold. Crude reverse-DCF intuition: a ~54x trailing multiple at a ~12–13% cost of equity only "makes sense" if earnings roughly **triple over ~3–4 years and then fade to mid-teens growth** — i.e. the price embeds the greenfield (Savli) ramp executing on time and exports scaling. It is **pricing in continued ~40–50% near-term growth**, not a normalized 20%. EV/EBITDA ~30x tells the same story and sits above Apar's ~21x.

## 4. Post-listing multiple history (de-rate vs re-rate)

Yash listed on **BSE SME on ~19 Dec 2024** at ₹146 (IPO P/E ~27x on FY24 EPS). The path since:
- Early 2025: brief de-rate, 52-wk low **₹150 (18 Mar 2025)** — essentially flat to IPO.
- Mid-2025 onward: sharp **re-rate**, 52-wk high **₹873**; P/E expanded to ~54–58x by Sep 2025–May 2026 (third-party trackers: 54.4x Sep-2025, 57.8x Mar-2026, 58.1x May-2026).

So the dominant move since listing is a **re-rating from ~27x to ~54–58x — roughly a doubling of the multiple** — driven by the H1 FY26 ~78% revenue surge and the bushing-shortage narrative. The stock is *not* de-rated; it sits near the top of its post-listing valuation range and Trendlyne flags an "Expensive Valuation" score; MoneyWorks4Me/ValueResearch flag it trading above its 3-yr historic valuation band.

## 5. Brokerage / analyst targets

**None of institutional quality.** Trendlyne: "No estimates available." No mainstream brokerage (Motilal, Kotak, ICICI, etc.) publishes a price target — consistent with SME-platform status and thin float. The only numeric "targets" surfaced are from non-credible forecasting blogs and are not usable. **Confidence that no reliable analyst fair-value exists: high.**

## 6. Sensitivity if growth normalizes to ~20–25%

This is the central risk to the thesis. Illustrative, not a forecast:
- A ~54x P/E on a business compounding ~20–25% implies **PEG ~2.2–2.7** — clearly demanding by conventional standards. High-quality 20–25% growers in this space (Voltamp, TARIL) trade at **30–36x**.
- If the market re-rated Yash toward a ~30–35x "normal-grower" multiple while earnings still grew, the **multiple compression alone is ~35–45%** — a meaningful headwind that strong earnings would have to outrun.
- The asymmetry: at this multiple the price is underwritten by execution of the Savli greenfield ramp and export scaling. Any slip to 20–25% growth, or margin give-back as the bushing shortage eases, would likely trigger de-rating, since there is little valuation cushion (P/B ~11x leaves no asset-value support).
- Conversely, if 40–50% growth *persists* for 2–3 more years, today's 54x compresses rapidly on forward earnings and the entry looks far less stretched — which is precisely what bulls are paying for.

## Synthesis — demanding, fair, or cheap?

On a **relative basis, the valuation is demanding.** Against its truest comparables — Shilchar (~29x, higher ROCE), TARIL/Voltamp (~32–36x), HBL (~26x, faster TTM growth, far higher ROCE) — Yash's ~54x P/E and ~11x P/B is a **substantial premium not fully explained by superior returns**; its ROCE/OPM are good but not best-in-class. It only looks "mid-pack" when benchmarked against large-cap T&D names (Hitachi 149x, CG 117x, GVT&D 99x), which are poor like-for-like comps for an SME.

On a **growth-adjusted basis the read is conditional.** Using trailing ~50%+ growth, PEG ~0.9–1.1 is reasonable; using management's own forward ~35–40% guidance, PEG ~1.4–1.5 is full; and on a normalized 20–25%, PEG ~2.2–2.7 is expensive. The multiple is effectively a **bet that 40%+ growth and ~24% margins persist through the greenfield/export ramp.** That is plausible given a cited structural HV-bushing shortage and a ~₹300–400 Cr order book, but it is an execution-dependent bet with little margin of safety.

**Bottom line (no recommendation):** The stock has re-rated, not de-rated, since its Dec-2024 listing and now trades near the top of its history and at a clear premium to its closest small-cap peers. The premium is **justified only if elevated growth and margins continue**; it is **not justified by current returns relative to Shilchar/HBL**, and it carries **high sensitivity to any growth normalization** (~35–45% potential multiple compression toward peer norms). No credible analyst fair-value exists to triangulate against — a notable information gap for a name at this valuation.
